---
title: "Low Total Cholesterol, Low Serum Albumin, and Elevated Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase as Independent Mortality Risk Markers Spanning a Fivefold Range in Mortality Rate: A Survey-Weighted Analysis of NHANES 1999–2018"
author: "David Winsemius"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  word_document:
    reference_docx: null
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

---

## Revision Log

| Rev | Date       | Changes |
|-----|------------|---------|
| 1   | 2026-05-29 | Initial full draft: Introduction, Methods, Results (Tables 2, 3a, 3b), Discussion with EHR scoring section, 17 Vancouver references |
| 2   | 2026-05-30 | Added structured BMJ abstract; fixed American spellings throughout (non-institutionalized, linearization, hypoalbuminemia, generalizability, venipuncture); split citation [14] — NHANES Weighting Tutorial extracted to new reference [18] |

---

## Abstract

**Objective** To determine whether three routine laboratory values — total cholesterol
(TC), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), and serum albumin — function as near-orthogonal
mortality risk predictors in adults free of statin use, and to evaluate a simple three-item
adverse marker score derived from their extreme quintiles.

**Design** Population-based cohort study with two-year landmark analysis.

**Setting** Ten consecutive two-year cycles of the National Health and Nutrition
Examination Survey (NHANES), 1999–2018, linked to the National Center for Health
Statistics Public-Use Linked Mortality Files.

**Participants** 32,792 non-statin users aged ≥18 years eligible for mortality
follow-up, alive and uncensored two years after examination (2,671 deaths over a mean
7.1 years of post-landmark follow-up).

**Main outcome measures** All-cause mortality rate (deaths per 1,000 person-years
after the two-year landmark); adjusted rate ratios from survey-weighted Poisson
regression; cause-specific mortality (cardiovascular, cancer, other).

**Results** Spearman rank correlations among TC, GGT, and albumin were near-zero to
weak (TC–albumin r = −0.004; GGT–albumin r = 0.05; TC–GGT r = 0.20), confirming
near-statistical independence. Each variable independently predicted all-cause
mortality: adjusted rate ratios for the bottom quintile of TC (≤161 mg/dL) 1.42
(95% CI 1.23–1.64), top quintile of GGT (≥33 U/L) 1.56 (1.41–1.73), and bottom
quintile of albumin (≤4.0 g/dL) 1.35 (1.23–1.49). A three-item adverse marker score
(0–3 points, one per adverse threshold) spanned a fivefold range in crude mortality
rate: 6.6, 8.5, 14.9, and 32.4 deaths per 1,000 person-years for scores 0 through 3;
all levels above zero remained significant after demographic and metabolic adjustment
(score 3 rate ratio 3.44, 95% CI 2.44–4.85). Three-way cross-classification revealed
a 57-fold difference in crude mortality between the lowest-risk combination (high
albumin + low GGT + low TC: 0.7/1,000 person-years, N=391) and the highest-risk
combination (low albumin + high GGT + low TC: 40.2/1,000 person-years, N=292), both
sharing low total cholesterol. Cancer mortality showed disproportionate excess at
score 3, beyond multiplicative expectation.

**Conclusions** In non-statin users, low TC, elevated GGT, and low serum albumin are
near-orthogonal predictors of mortality derivable from a single routine blood panel.
A three-item adverse marker score requiring no additional testing spans a fivefold
mortality gradient and resolves the low-cholesterol paradox: low TC carries no excess
risk in the absence of concurrent metabolic stress markers, but identifies patients at
substantially elevated risk when GGT and albumin are also adverse. The score is
directly computable from structured data already present in electronic health records.

---

## Introduction

The association between low serum total cholesterol and excess all-cause mortality has
been reported in population cohorts for four decades, yet it remains poorly integrated
into clinical practice.[1,2] Cardiologists and primary care physicians encounter low
cholesterol as an ostensibly reassuring finding — one that signals freedom from
atherosclerotic risk — and rarely pursue it further. The epidemiological literature tells
a more complicated story: below roughly 160 mg/dL, mortality rates rise in a pattern that
is neither explained by cardiovascular disease nor accounted for by the conventional
lipid-CHD narrative.[3,4]

The standard explanation invokes reverse causation: subclinical malignancy, hepatic
dysfunction, and protein-energy malnutrition each suppress cholesterol synthesis before
they announce themselves clinically, so low cholesterol marks pre-existing disease rather
than an independent causal exposure.[5,6] This explanation is almost certainly correct as
far as it goes. The clinical question it leaves unanswered is more specific: which
measurable dimensions of systemic illness are responsible, and can identifying them
prospectively separate the patient whose low cholesterol reflects constitutional leanness
or efficient hepatic metabolism from the one whose low cholesterol reflects catabolic
depletion? Without that discrimination, the clinician confronting a low cholesterol result
has no basis for action. What is needed is not a refutation of reverse causation but
practical, targeted precision — routine measurements that locate the patient within the
illness-depletion spectrum at the time of the blood draw.

Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) offers one such mechanistic lens. Conventionally understood
as a marker of alcohol exposure and hepatobiliary disease, GGT also reflects intracellular
glutathione turnover and serves as a sensitive index of oxidative stress at concentrations
well within the conventional normal range.[7,8] Several cohort studies have reported GGT
as an independent all-cause and cardiovascular mortality predictor,[9,10] and its
association with lipid peroxidation provides a biological rationale for interaction with
cholesterol metabolism. Serum albumin provides a complementary axis: reflecting both
nutritional reserve and hepatic synthetic capacity, it falls in catabolic and inflammatory
states and predicts mortality across a concentration range that laboratory reports
routinely label as normal.[11,12]

We examined whether total cholesterol, GGT, and serum albumin function as orthogonal
prognostic dimensions — statistically independent of one another yet jointly predictive
of mortality — in a nationally representative population free of statin use at enrollment.
Using ten consecutive cycles of the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey
(NHANES, 1999–2018) linked to the National Center for Health Statistics Public-Use
Linked Mortality Files,[13] we applied a two-year landmark to attenuate reverse
causation, excluded statin users to remove pharmacological confounding of cholesterol
levels, and modeled all-cause and cause-specific mortality using survey-weighted Poisson
regression. We further derived a simple three-item adverse marker score — one point each
for low cholesterol, elevated GGT, and low albumin — and assessed its clinical
discriminative utility across a fivefold gradient in mortality rate. Our findings suggest
that the prognostic significance of low total cholesterol is not an artefact of reverse
causation but a marker of a specific pathophysiological state, identifiable at the time of
a routine blood draw, that warrants clinical evaluation rather than reassurance.

---

## Methods

### Study population

The National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) is a continuous,
cross-sectional survey of the non-institutionalized United States civilian population,
conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) using a complex
multistage probability sampling design.[14] We pooled ten two-year cycles spanning
1999–2018. Participants who attended the Mobile Examination Center (MEC) and were aged
18 years or older at the time of survey were eligible. We excluded participants currently
using lipid-lowering medications (statins) at enrollment, identified from the
prescription medication interview files (RXQ_RX); drug names containing "statin" but
not "nystatin" were classified as statin use. Participants were further restricted to
those eligible for mortality follow-up (ELIGSTAT = 1) in the NCHS Public-Use Linked
Mortality Files, which provide vital status through December 31, 2019.[13]

To attenuate reverse causation — the tendency for subclinical terminal illness to lower
cholesterol, GGT, and albumin before clinical presentation — we applied a two-year
landmark: participants who died or were censored within two years of their examination
date were excluded, and follow-up time was reset to begin at the two-year mark. The
analysis population after these exclusions comprised 32,792 participants with 2,671
deaths.

### Exposures

Serum total cholesterol (TC, mg/dL), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT, U/L), and serum
albumin (g/dL) were measured in fasting and non-fasting MEC participants as part of the
standard NHANES laboratory protocol. GGT and albumin were obtained from the standard
biochemistry profile (file series LAB18, L40, and BIOPRO across cycles). Laboratory
file names changed across cycles; file-to-variable linkage across all ten cycles was
managed using the nhanesR package for R.[15]

An adverse marker score (0–3) was constructed by summing three binary flags, each
derived from the bottom or top quintile of the respective variable in the analysis
population: low TC (≤ 161 mg/dL), elevated GGT (≥ 33 U/L), and low albumin
(≤ 4.0 g/dL). Quintile thresholds were fixed at values derived from the full analysis
population prior to any modelling.

### Outcomes

The primary outcome was all-cause mortality ascertained through linkage to the NCHS
Public-Use Linked Mortality Files, with follow-up through December 31, 2019. Cause of
death was classified using the leading cause recode provided in the mortality files
(UCOD_LEADING): cardiovascular mortality (codes 001 and 005), cancer mortality (code
002), and all other causes. Participants alive at December 31, 2019 were censored at
that date.

### Statistical analysis

All analyses accounted for the NHANES complex survey design using Taylor-series
linearization, with primary sampling units (SDMVPSU), strata (SDMVSTRA), and
examination sample weights as specified by NCHS analytic guidelines.[14] Following NCHS
recommendations for pooled multi-cycle analyses, examination weights were adjusted to
represent the full 1999–2018 study period. Because the 1999–2000 cycle used pre-Census
2000 population controls while subsequent cycles used post-Census counts, a direct
division of WTMEC2YR by the number of cycles is not appropriate for analyses including
1999–2000.[18] The pre-computed four-year weight (WTMEC4YR) was therefore used for the
1999–2002 block, and the adjusted pooled weight was defined as WTMEC_adj = (2/10) ×
WTMEC4YR for cycles 1999–2002, and (1/10) × WTMEC2YR for cycles 2003–2018, where 10
is the total number of two-year cycles combined. Singleton PSUs within strata arising
from subgroup analyses were handled by the "adjust" method.

Mortality rates were expressed as deaths per 1,000 person-years of follow-up after the
two-year landmark. Survey-weighted Poisson regression (quasipoisson family, log link)
with an offset of log(follow-up time in years) was used to estimate adjusted rate ratios
(RR) and 95% confidence intervals. Models were adjusted for age (continuous), sex,
race/ethnicity (five categories), poverty-income ratio (continuous), body mass index
(continuous), and smoking status (never, former, current). Total cholesterol, GGT, and
albumin were each entered as three-level categorical variables — bottom quintile (Low),
middle three quintiles (Mid, reference), and top quintile (High) — yielding six
parameters for the three exposure terms. The adverse marker score was entered as an
unordered factor with score 0 as reference.

Cause-specific mortality was modeled using the same Poisson framework, treating deaths
from other causes as censored at the date of death. The Spearman correlations among TC,
GGT, and albumin were computed to assess the orthogonality assumption. All analyses were
performed in R (version 4.6)[16] using the survey package (version 4.4).[17]

---

## Results

### Analysis population

After exclusion of statin users, participants ineligible for mortality follow-up,
and those who died or were censored within two years of examination, the analysis
population comprised 32,792 participants contributing 2,671 deaths over a mean of
7.1 years of post-landmark follow-up. Median total cholesterol was 193 mg/dL
(IQR 166–221), median GGT 19 U/L (IQR 13–29), and median serum albumin 4.3 g/dL
(IQR 4.0–4.5) — all within conventional laboratory reference ranges.

### Independence of the three exposure variables

Spearman rank correlations among the three exposures were: TC–GGT r = 0.20,
TC–albumin r = −0.004, GGT–albumin r = 0.05. In the six-parameter Poisson model,
adjusted rate ratios for each variable were stable when the other two were included,
indicating that each contributes independent predictive information.

### Dose-response by decile

**Table 2. Survey-weighted all-cause mortality rates by decile of each exposure
(non-statin users, 2-year landmark, N=32,767)**

*Total Cholesterol (mg/dL)*

| Decile | Range   |     N | Events | Rate/1000py |
|--------|---------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1      | 59–146  | 3,094 |    180 |         6.7 |
| 2      | 146–161 | 3,004 |    189 |         6.9 |
| 3      | 161–172 | 2,936 |    184 |         6.7 |
| 4      | 172–183 | 3,089 |    203 |         7.0 |
| 5      | 183–193 | 2,965 |    243 |         7.9 |
| 6      | 193–203 | 2,864 |    229 |         7.4 |
| 7      | 203–215 | 3,089 |    256 |         7.8 |
| 8      | 215–229 | 2,905 |    283 |        10.3 |
| 9      | 229–250 | 2,931 |    318 |        10.7 |
| 10     | 250–813 | 2,890 |    313 |        10.5 |

*GGT (U/L)*

| Decile | Range    |     N | Events | Rate/1000py |
|--------|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1      | 3–10     | 3,361 |    160 |         5.0 |
| 2      | 10–12    | 2,707 |    180 |         6.7 |
| 3      | 12–14    | 2,924 |    233 |         8.5 |
| 4      | 14–17    | 4,199 |    307 |         7.4 |
| 5      | 17–19    | 2,346 |    204 |         8.5 |
| 6      | 19–22    | 2,775 |    210 |         8.4 |
| 7      | 22–26    | 2,811 |    217 |         7.3 |
| 8      | 26–33    | 2,913 |    229 |         7.8 |
| 9      | 33–48    | 2,846 |    258 |         9.8 |
| 10     | 48–2274  | 2,885 |    400 |        15.2 |

*Serum Albumin (g/dL)*

| Decile | Range   |     N | Events | Rate/1000py |
|--------|---------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1      | 1.9–3.8 | 3,638 |    485 |        15.8 |
| 2      | 3.8–4.0 | 4,290 |    469 |        11.9 |
| 3      | 4.0–4.1 | 3,049 |    294 |        10.6 |
| 4      | 4.1–4.2 | 3,442 |    294 |         8.3 |
| 5      | 4.2–4.3 | 3,434 |    241 |         7.5 |
| 6      | 4.3–4.4 | 3,306 |    240 |         7.5 |
| 7      | 4.4–4.5 | 2,881 |    154 |         5.5 |
| 8      | 4.5–4.7 | 3,700 |    166 |         4.6 |
| 9      | 4.7–5.6 | 2,027 |     55 |         3.0 |

### Adverse marker scores

Two complementary scoring systems were derived from the same three exposures.
The 0–9 score assigns 0–3 points per variable based on quartile position in the
adverse direction (TC: lowest quartile = 3, highest = 0; GGT: highest quartile = 3,
lowest = 0; albumin: lowest quartile = 3, highest = 0). The 0–3 binary score awards
one point per variable for crossing the bottom or top quintile threshold.

**Table 3a. Survey-weighted mortality rates and adjusted rate ratios: 0–9 score
(ref = score 0)**

Adjusted for age, sex, race/ethnicity, poverty-income ratio, BMI, and smoking status.

| Score |     N | Events | Rate/1000py | Adjusted RR (95% CI)  |
|-------|-------|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| 0     |   164 |     11 |         6.8 | 1.00 (reference)       |
| 1     |   713 |     44 |         6.4 | 0.90 (0.41–1.96)       |
| 2     | 2,098 |    147 |         6.4 | 0.84 (0.45–1.57)       |
| 3     | 4,992 |    338 |         6.8 | 0.91 (0.49–1.68)       |
| 4     | 6,137 |    423 |         7.0 | 1.05 (0.56–1.95)       |
| 5     | 6,327 |    505 |         8.4 | 1.17 (0.63–2.16)       |
| 6     | 4,973 |    420 |         8.5 | 1.16 (0.62–2.17)       |
| 7     | 2,652 |    252 |        11.3 | 1.42 (0.74–2.75)       |
| 8     | 1,263 |    161 |        16.8 | 1.95 (1.04–3.66)       |
| 9     |   448 |     97 |        28.0 | 2.56 (1.30–5.07)       |

**Table 3b. Survey-weighted mortality rates and adjusted rate ratios: 0–3 binary
score (ref = score 0)**

One point each for TC ≤161 mg/dL, GGT ≥33 U/L, albumin ≤4.0 g/dL (bottom/top
quintile thresholds). Adjusted for age, sex, race/ethnicity, poverty-income ratio,
BMI, and smoking status.

| Score |      N | Events | Rate/1000py | Adjusted RR (95% CI) |
|-------|--------|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| 0     | 13,320 |    912 |         6.6 | 1.00 (reference)     |
| 1     | 13,110 |  1,031 |         8.5 | 1.48 (1.33–1.64)     |
| 2     |  3,031 |    379 |        14.9 | 1.99 (1.73–2.29)     |
| 3     |    306 |     76 |        32.4 | 3.44 (2.44–4.85)     |

The discriminative range of the score is illustrated by its extremes in the full
three-way cross-classification (Figure 1). Participants with high albumin, low GGT,
and low TC — the cell that exonerates low cholesterol in the absence of systemic
illness markers — had a crude mortality rate of 0.7 per 1,000 person-years (N=391,
2 deaths). Participants with low albumin, high GGT, and low TC had a rate of 40.2
(N=292, 73 deaths). Both groups share a low total cholesterol; the 57-fold
difference in mortality rate is explained entirely by GGT and albumin.

### Cause-specific mortality

On cause-specific analysis, the association of score 3 with cancer mortality was
disproportionately large relative to the product of the marginal risks (observed
joint rate ratio 1.90, 95% CI 1.25–2.88; expected under multiplicativity 1.13),
suggesting that the combination of depleted cholesterol synthesis, oxidative hepatic
stress, and hypoalbuminemia marks a state of advanced occult malignancy more
specifically than any single marker alone. The association with cardiovascular
mortality was closer to multiplicative expectations.

---

## Discussion

In a nationally representative cohort of US adults free of statin use at enrollment,
three routine laboratory values — low total cholesterol, elevated GGT, and low serum
albumin — each independently predicted all-cause mortality after a two-year landmark,
with Spearman rank correlations among them ranging from near zero (TC–albumin:
r = −0.004; GGT–albumin: r = 0.05) to weak (TC–GGT: r = 0.20). The three variables
capture largely non-overlapping biological terrain: lipid synthetic capacity, oxidative
and hepatic metabolic stress, and nutritional-inflammatory reserve. A simple adverse
marker score derived from their extreme quintiles spanned a fivefold range in
mortality rate — from 6.6 to 32.4 deaths per 1,000 person-years — with all three
levels above zero retaining significance after adjustment for standard demographic and
metabolic covariates.

The weak positive rank correlation between TC and GGT (r = 0.20) is biologically
interpretable: both tend to rise in the context of metabolic syndrome and insulin
resistance, representing partially shared metabolic terrain. That this correlation
is modest rather than strong is important — it means the two variables are not
interchangeable, and that a person with high GGT and low TC occupies a pathophysiological
space that neither marker alone identifies. The pattern of the 0–9 score is instructive
in this regard: risk is essentially flat across scores 0 through 4, then rises sharply
and monotonically from score 7 onward. This threshold character suggests that the
mortality signal emerges when adverse values co-occur across multiple axes, not when
any single axis is mildly abnormal.

The finding that low total cholesterol in the presence of high albumin and low GGT
carries a mortality rate of 0.7 per 1,000 person-years — lower than any other
combination — resolves the clinical paradox rather than merely attenuating it. The
patient whose low cholesterol reflects constitutional leanness, efficient hepatic
metabolism, or dietary pattern is not at elevated risk. The patient whose low
cholesterol co-occurs with hypoalbuminemia and elevated GGT is at substantially
elevated risk, consistent with occult malignancy, chronic liver disease, or advanced
protein-energy wasting. The cause-specific analysis supports this: the excess cancer
mortality associated with the low-TC/high-GGT combination exceeded multiplicative
expectation, consistent with cholesterol depletion by a tumour burden that also
drives oxidative hepatic stress.

### Clinical implications

The practical consequence for the clinician is straightforward: a low total cholesterol
result in isolation warrants no action beyond routine follow-up. The same result in a
patient with an adverse marker score of 2 or 3 — identifiable from the same blood
draw — warrants clinical evaluation for occult systemic disease. This is a risk
stratification, not a causal claim; the score identifies who needs looking at, not
what to look for.

All three components of the score are elements of panels already ordered in routine
clinical care — the lipid panel (TC) and the comprehensive metabolic panel (GGT,
albumin) are among the most frequently performed laboratory tests in ambulatory
medicine. The adverse marker score requires no additional testing and no proprietary
reagents. It is directly computable from structured laboratory data already stored in
electronic health records, making it a natural candidate for automated clinical
decision support: a flag triggered when a patient's lab results meet two or more
adverse thresholds could prompt the ordering clinician to consider further evaluation
before the result would otherwise be filed and forgotten. Implementation within
existing EHR rule engines — such as Best Practice Advisories in Epic or equivalent
systems — would require only the three threshold values and a simple count.

### Limitations

Several limitations warrant consideration. Exposures were measured at a single
examination; transient illness at the time of the MEC visit could simultaneously
depress TC and albumin and elevate GGT, mimicking the adverse score pattern through
acute-phase physiology rather than chronic disease burden. The two-year landmark
attenuates but does not eliminate this possibility. GGT is sensitive to recent alcohol
consumption and biliary obstruction as well as oxidative stress, and NHANES does not
provide sufficient alcohol detail to fully disentangle these contributors. Albumin is
affected by hydration status and posture at venipuncture. The score thresholds were
derived from the analysis population itself and require external validation before
clinical implementation. Finally, statin exclusion, while necessary to avoid
pharmacological confounding of TC, limits generalizability to the substantial
proportion of middle-aged and older adults currently taking lipid-lowering therapy.

---

## References

<!-- Vancouver-style numbered references. Items marked [VERIFY] should be confirmed
     before submission — author, year, and journal are as recalled but page numbers
     and volume should be checked against PubMed. -->

1. Jacobs D, Blackburn H, Higgins M, et al. Report of the Conference on Low Blood
   Cholesterol: Mortality Associations. *Circulation*. 1992;86(3):1046–60.

2. Neaton JD, Blackburn H, Jacobs D, et al. Serum cholesterol level and mortality
   findings for men screened in the Multiple Risk Factor Intervention Trial.
   *Arch Intern Med*. 1992;152(7):1490–500.

3. Zhou L, Guo X, Yu S, et al. The effect of total cholesterol/high-density lipoprotein
   cholesterol ratio on mortality risk in the general population.
   *Front Endocrinol (Lausanne)*. 2022;13:1037573. [PMC9797665]

4. Liu Y, Tong G, Tong W, Lu L, Qin X. Can statin reduce the J-curve effect of
   cholesterol and all-cause mortality? An analysis including 467,153 participants.
   *Sci Rep*. 2021;11(1):22521. [VERIFY volume/page]

5. Iribarren C, Reed DM, Chen R, Yano K, Dwyer JH. Low serum cholesterol and mortality:
   which is the cause and which is the effect? *Circulation*. 1995;92(9):2396–403.

6. Schatz IJ, Masaki K, Yano K, Chen R, Rodriguez BL, Curb JD. Cholesterol and
   all-cause mortality in elderly people from the Honolulu Heart Program: a cohort study.
   *Lancet*. 2001;358(9279):351–5.

7. Pompella A, Corti A, Paolicchi A, Giommarelli C, Zunino F. Gamma-glutamyltransferase,
   redox regulation and cancer drug resistance. *Curr Opin Pharmacol*.
   2007;7(4):360–6. [VERIFY]

8. Lee DH, Jacobs DR Jr. Serum gamma-glutamyltransferase: new insights about an old
   marker of risk. *Curr Opin Lipidol*. 2009;20(3):198–203.

9. Ruttmann E, Brant LJ, Concin H, Diem G, Rapp K, Ulmer H; Vorarlberg Health
   Monitoring and Promotion Program Study Group. Gamma-glutamyltransferase as a risk
   factor for cardiovascular disease mortality: an epidemiological investigation in a
   cohort of 163,944 Austrian adults. *Circulation*. 2005;112(14):2130–7.

10. Wannamethee G, Ebrahim S, Shaper AG. Gamma-glutamyltransferase: determinants and
    association with mortality from ischemic heart disease and all causes.
    *Am J Epidemiol*. 1995;142(7):699–708.

11. Corti MC, Guralnik JM, Salive ME, Sorkin JD. Serum albumin level and physical
    disability as predictors of mortality in older persons.
    *JAMA*. 1994;272(13):1036–42.

12. Don BR, Kaysen G. Serum albumin: relationship to inflammation and nutrition.
    *Semin Dial*. 2004;17(6):432–7.

13. National Center for Health Statistics. Public-use linked mortality files, 2019.
    Hyattsville, MD: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; 2021.
    https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data-linkage/mortality-public.htm

14. National Center for Health Statistics. NHANES analytic and reporting guidelines.
    Hyattsville, MD: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; 2013.
    https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/analyticguidelines.aspx

15. Winsemius D. nhanesR: download, harmonise, and link NHANES survey data in R.
    R package version 0.1.0. 2025. https://github.com/dwinsemius/nhanesR

16. R Core Team. R: A language and environment for statistical computing.
    Vienna, Austria: R Foundation for Statistical Computing; 2025.
    https://www.R-project.org/

17. Lumley T. Analysis of complex survey samples. *J Stat Softw*. 2004;9(1):1–19.

18. National Center for Health Statistics. NHANES Tutorials — Module 6: Weighting.
    Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Available at:
    https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/tutorials/weighting.aspx. Accessed 2026.
